title: Pet Grief Is Real Grief
subtitle: Why the loss of an animal deserves the same space and respect as any other bereavement
excerpt: A reading about pet loss and why the grief is often larger than the world around you expects. Covers what makes animal loss distinctive, the social permission problem, and what actually helps.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. Draws on the psychology of attachment and the research on animal-human bonds.

practice.whenToUse: Use after losing a pet, particularly when the grief feels larger than the world around you seems to expect.

content.text: The grief of losing a pet does not register on most official scales of loss. There is no standard time off work for it. The bereavement counsellor does not phone. The flowers do not arrive. And yet the loss can be one of the most destabilising experiences a person goes through, particularly when the animal was a primary source of company or comfort.
What the relationship actually was

For many people, a pet is not simply a pet. The animal was the one who was always there. The one who did not have opinions about your life choices. The one who waited at the door. The one who slept next to you during the years when other forms of comfort were not available.

Animals do not ask anything of us except presence. They do not need us to perform wellness or manage how they see us. For people who have spent their lives careful about how they appear to others, this kind of unconditional company is not a small thing. It is, for some, the most uncomplicated relationship they have ever had.
Why the grief can feel disproportionate

Pet grief sometimes surprises people with its intensity. A fish, a hamster, a small bird: the size of the creature does not determine the size of the loss. What determines the size of the loss is the role that animal played in daily life and how regularly they were present in it.

Grief is, at its root, the loss of something that organised your world. When the cat was part of the morning routine and the evening routine and every quiet moment in between, the loss is not just the loss of a cat. It is the loss of the structure the cat held.

The grief can also be complicated by the circumstances of the death. Pets often die by decision rather than by accident or illness running its own course. Being responsible for the timing of an end-of-life choice adds its own particular weight. The question of whether it was too early or too late can follow people for a long time. This is a very common feature of pet grief, and it is not a sign that the decision was wrong.
The social permission problem

One of the specific difficulties of pet grief is the limited social permission to grieve fully. People may be sympathetic, but the sympathy tends to be brief. "Did you get a new one yet?" is often asked within days. The message, rarely intended but often received, is: this loss does not warrant extended grieving.

This can push the grief underground. The person continues to grieve but stops speaking about it. The combination of active grief and nowhere to put it is its own particular difficulty. It is not the grief that becomes the problem. It is the isolation of the grief.
What grief for an animal is like

The pattern of pet grief follows the same irregular terrain as any significant loss. There are days when the absence is everywhere. There are days that feel almost normal. There are specific triggers: the food bowl that is still there, the lead on the hook, the habit of looking toward their usual spot.

Some people feel the absence most in the morning, before the day has started and the mind is still in the old routines. Others feel it most in the evenings. Some feel it only in unexpected moments: reaching for the lead, or setting down food for a pet that is no longer there.
What helps

There is no timetable for pet grief and no method that reliably shortens it. What tends to help is the same thing that helps in any grief: acknowledgement. The loss deserves to be named and felt, even when the people around you do not have a framework for it.
Some people find it useful to mark the loss in some way: a small ceremony, a photograph framed, a plant in the garden. This is not excessive. This is what humans have always done with loss.

Some people find it useful to talk about the animal, to tell the story of what they were like. Others prefer to keep it private. Some find a new animal helps. Others find the idea of a new animal unbearable for a long time. Both are normal responses, and neither is the right answer for everyone.
When the grief lingers

Pet grief can last a long time, particularly when the animal was a primary companion during a difficult period of life. There is no point at which grief becomes a problem to be solved rather than a process to be moved through. The timeline is different for everyone.

If the grief is significantly affecting daily functioning for an extended period, it is worth speaking to someone who can sit with it alongside you. Not because the grief is wrong, but because some grief benefits from not being carried alone.
Where this practice comes from
Original to homemade.education. It draws on research into the bonds between people and animals.
